rationale: The subject property (orange dashed outline) in Spring, TX displays a complex multi-hip roof with several intersecting hip planes and what appear to be small hip-dormer protrusions near the front. Key visual cues: (1) Shadow depth — the dark shadow valleys between intersecting hip planes are moderately prominent, consistent with a mid-range pitch rather than a shallow 4:12 or steep 9:12+; (2) Roof-to-eave geometry — the roof planes are clearly visible from overhead and occupy a notable portion of the plan footprint, but the hip returns are not razor-thin, pointing to a moderate slope; (3) Ridge prominence — multiple ridgelines are visible with clear directional shading on opposing faces, suggesting enough vertical rise to differentiate illuminated vs. shadowed planes; (4) Regional context — this is a suburban Houston/Spring, TX neighborhood where 6:12 is the dominant architectural pitch for this style of multi-hip luxury home; neighboring homes exhibit nearly identical roof profiles reinforcing this estimate. Taken together, the evidence best supports a 6:12 pitch, with 7:12 as a plausible alternative if the home leans toward the upper end of the local norm.
